research_answer: The data shows a clear and counterintuitive pattern: raw sugar exporters are far more likely to develop exports in garments — an apparently unrelated sector — than in confectionery, which is the direct downstream product of sugar. At any given point, two-thirds of raw sugar exporters also export apparel, while only one-third export confectionery. Even more striking, when looking at transitions over a 5-year window, only 3% of raw sugar exporters who lacked confectionery exports in 1995 developed them by 2000, compared to 30% who transitioned into garments. This implies that the productive capabilities needed to export raw sugar (e.g., tropical climate, agricultural labor, basic processing) are more compatible with labor-intensive garment manufacturing than with the food processing skills required for confectionery.
supporting_quotes: Only one third of raw sugar exporters also export that natural resource's downstream industry: confectionary products. But two thirds export manufactured apparel.
Only 3% of raw sugar exporters who did not export confectionary products in 1995 were able to discover that sector by 2000, whereas the percentage of transitions from sugar to garments was 30%.
